chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame below allocators/sanitizers: stack shows only abort, several unknown frames, then Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString, so that JNI function is the first relevant app frame.

Tooling access to decompile libheresdk.so or Java code is mandated, but the crash record does not provide concrete native instruction context, arguments, or proof of memory corruption, only that the process called abort.

The JNI entry is Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString, but the reported JNI bridge method in the crash metadata is java.util.UUID.fromString, which is pure Java in the standard library and not mapped to libheresdk.so; therefore the mapping is likely inaccurate or the harness associated the crash to the wrong method.

The Java call graph shows com.inmobi.media.ir$3.run -> java.util.UUID.fromString; the fuzzer entry is fuzz_one_input, which then reaches Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString on the native side according to the stack trace. There is no evidence that UUID.fromString is actually implemented in native code in this app.
Abort-style termination without allocator/sanitizer frames suggests an explicit abort() or failed internal assertion rather than immediate evidence of out-of-bounds access or use-after-free.

Without decompiled code or arguments, there is no proof that untrusted input can directly control any unsafe memory operation inside Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString; for example, it might just validate the input string, detect an invalid format and call abort, which is a logic/robustness bug but not necessarily an exploitable vulnerability.

The fuzzer may also be feeding unrealistic or malformed data into a native API in a way that the real app never would (e.g., never calling GeoCoordinates.fromString with arbitrary fuzz bytes), which further reduces confidence that this is a reachable, attacker-controllable issue.

Given the limited stack and absence of sanitizer evidence and of any explicit memory-manipulation function in the visible frames, the most reasonable classification is that this is a non-diagnostic abort likely due to internal validation failure or harness misuse, not a confirmed memory-safety vulnerability.
reasons: Crash is an abort without visible sanitizer or allocator frames, indicating an explicit termination rather than clear memory corruption.
Only identified native frame is Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString; no evidence of memcpy/memmove or pointer arithmetic is available from the trace.
Java-side method java.util.UUID.fromString is standard library logic; mapping to the native libheresdk.so frame is inconsistent and suggests possible harness or symbol attribution issues.
recommendations: Manually decompile Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString in libheresdk.so to check for unsafe memory operations or unchecked assumptions about the input string format.
Instrument and re-run with AddressSanitizer or similar to determine if any hidden buffer overflows or invalid memory accesses occur before abort.
Validate whether real app control flows can pass untrusted or arbitrary data into GeoCoordinates.fromString; if not, consider constraining the fuzzer harness to realistic inputs.
assumptions: UUID.fromString call path in the Java call graph is not actually backed by native implementation in this app.
Abort was triggered by internal validation or assertion failure rather than low-level memory error.
Fuzzer may be invoking JNI methods with inputs not possible in normal app execution.
limitations: No decompiled native code for Java_com_here_sdk_core_GeoCoordinates_fromString was available to inspect for memory-unsafe operations.
No concrete register or argument state at the moment of abort is provided, preventing precise data-flow analysis.
Lack of allocator/sanitizer context or error logs makes it impossible to confirm or rule out subtle memory corruption prior to abort.
